next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 13

By the time Leela found the fourth-floor study room, the library had thinned to the kind of public quiet that made every ordinary sound feel indictable. Snowmelt squealed under her boots. The radiator under the window clicked and sighed. Inside the glass room June had already spread herself across the long table with the force of someone claiming a hospital chair: yellow legal pad open, laptop half charged, coat on the second seat, four folded nicotine-gum wrappers lined in a neat silver row by her wrist.

"You're late," June said without looking up.

"It is 7:04."

"That's what late means when I got here first."

Leela set down her backpack and the cardboard tray of coffees. Her right hand hurt inside the wrist brace. The cup lids had bitten damp rings into her palm. June took one, peeled back the cap, sniffed, and looked offended.

"This is decaf."

"It was the shortest line."

June finally looked at her, took in the brace, the wet hair escaping Leela's clip, whatever lived in her face after the Omar letter week, and let some sharper answer die. "Fine," she said. "We'll suffer in a dignified institutional way."

Omar arrived three minutes later with his coat buttoned to the throat and a clear bakery clamshell balanced on top of his laptop. Date bars, cut into exact rectangles, dusted with sugar that had already started to melt in the heat of the room. He set them down with an apologetic little lift of one shoulder.

"The man at Cedar Market has decided I'm no longer allowed to live on coffee," he said. "He was very severe about it."

"Good," June said. "Some...
open_hooks_to_carry: Let immigration paperwork remain legible and concrete rather than becoming abstract pressure.
Show Omar at a level of fear that strips away his usual elegance without reducing him to a victim-only role.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...fore he stood, as if open tabs could testify. Mohit carried his ceramic mug upstairs, where the real cups lived.

Leela remained at the table with the updated draft on her screen. The basement sounded normal again. Fans, fluorescent buzz, somebody in the far room laughing too loudly at nothing. She put her hands flat on the laminate and felt the tacky residue the lemon cleaner had left. Across the table, June's half-peeled muffin paper sat like evidence from a very small crime.

June came back for her gum wrapper. She saw Leela still there and stopped.

For a second Leela thought she might say it. Any version of it. That was a smoke run. You know it. Don't make me do this with you. Instead June picked the wrapper up, rolled it into a hard silver bead between finger and thumb, and said, "Your clause is doing a lot of lifting."

Leela looked at the sentence on the screen. "That was the point."

"Yeah." June tucked the bead into her pocket. "I know."

She left. No door slam, no clean accusation, no absolution. Just the small mechanical thud of the basement door and the hum rising to fill the space she had taken with her.

Leela saved the file, synced the folder, and closed the laptop. In the shared drive the paper sat under its revised name with the new caption locked in place, available to all of them, owned by all of them, deniable by none of them in any way that would matter. Nobody had said the word dishonest. They had used matched, scoped, clean, precise, mature. By noon those words belonged to the result, and because they belonged to the result, they belonged to the lab.
state_deltas_required: Omar needs a signed funding letter uploaded before his visa paperwork lapses.
Leela ghosts part of Mohit's letter and bribes Ben with prospective authorship to get the document filed, saving Omar temporarily while corrupting herself further.
